एतन्मे संशयं कृष्ण छेत्तुमर्हस्यशेषतः ।
त्वदन्यः संशयस्यास्य छेत्ता न ह्युपपद्यते ॥ ३९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
etan me saṁśayaṁ kṛṣṇa chettum arhasy aśeṣataḥ |
tvad-anyaḥ saṁśayasyāsya chettā na hy upapadyate || 39 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: This is my doubt, O Kṛṣṇa, and I ask You to dispel it completely. But for You, no one is to be found who can destroy this doubt.
This is my doubt, O Krishna, and I ask You to dispel it completely. But for You, no one is to be found who can destroy this doubt.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 853 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Kṛṣṇa is the perfect knower of past, present and future. In the beginning of the Bhagavad-gītā, the Lord said that all living entities existed individually in the past, they exist now in the present, and they continue to retain individual identity in the future, even after liberation from the material entanglement.

disagreements_among_translators.word: tvad-anyaḥ ... chettā na upapadyate (no one other than you can cut [this doubt])

positions.rendering: besides Yourself, there is no one who can destroy this doubt
only the omniscient teacher can dispel this doubt; no other has the standing
only the Lord can answer completely; no one else is qualified

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings preserve the structural claim that this specific question requires this specific authority to answer. The operational reach is shared: the question must be addressed by one who has crossed the path being asked about.

literal_meaning: This doubt of mine, O Krishna, you alone are competent to cut entirely — for no one other than you can dispel this doubt.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.39 closes the question-arc with a request to the teacher. The verb chettum (to cut) frames doubt as something to be severed — sharp, terminal, complete. Aśeṣataḥ — without remainder. Tvad-anyaḥ — other than you. The verse names that the answer requires the specific competence of the teacher who has himself crossed the path — no other competent answerer is available (na upapadyate). Sankara's reading: only the omniscient teacher can dispel this doubt; no other has the standing to cut it. Ramanuja's reading: only the Lord can answer this question completely; no one else is qualified. Both traditions preserve the structural claim: certain questions can only be answered by those who have themselves traversed what is being asked about. This pre-figures 4.34's tad viddhi praṇipātena — approach the teacher who has seen, with humility — and is itself a model of the right disposition for receiving the answer that follows.
